_notes: Essays are argumentative, idea-driven pieces ГѓВѓГ‚ВѓГѓВ‚Г‚ВўГѓВѓГ‚В‚ГѓВ‚Г‚ВЂГѓВѓГ‚В‚ГѓВ‚Г‚В” almost always Phronesis originals
category: governance | technology | law | society ГѓВѓГ‚ВѓГѓВ‚Г‚ВўГѓВѓГ‚В‚ГѓВ‚Г‚ВЂГѓВѓГ‚В‚ГѓВ‚Г‚В” used for the topic filter
excerpt: 2-4 sentence pull-quote style teaser shown in spotlight and grid views (can differ from abstract)

entries.title: Pausing isn't policy
entries.abstract: This essay examines Senator Bernie Sanders' recent legislation proposing a federal moratorium on data center construction, arguing that while grounded in legitimate AI safety concerns, the policy approach is fundamentally flawed. The author contends that data center moratoria cannot effectively constrain AI development due to the fungibility of computing infrastructure and the global nature of AI capability buildout, citing examples from Singapore and the Middle East. Furthermore, the author argues that the moratorium's conditionsвЂ”encompassing labor protections, environmental standards, and export controlsвЂ”constitute an overly broad policy package that conflates distinct governance challenges and represents an ineffective use of limited U.S. leverage over chip supply chains.

entries.title: Anthropic Settles Its Copyright LitigationвЂ”and Why That Was the Right Move
entries.abstract: This essay examines Anthropic's settlement of copyright litigation with book authors for $1.5 billion, arguing that despite the substantial payment, settling was strategically sound for the company. The analysis explains how the settlement leaves Anthropic's existing models intact while establishing a pathway for continued legal data acquisition through used book purchases. The essay contextualizes the settlement within broader copyright-AI litigation trends, compares compensation rates to industry standards, and identifies key remaining legal risks for model creators.

entries.title: Model Context Protocol (MCP) Architecture overview
entries.subtitle: Technical documentation explaining the Model Context Protocol's scope, core concepts, and layered architecture for context exchange between AI applications and servers.
entries.abstract: This documentation provides a comprehensive overview of the Model Context Protocol (MCP), a specification for enabling AI applications to exchange context with external servers. The document outlines MCP's scope, including its specification, SDKs, development tools, and reference implementations, and explains the protocol's client-server architecture where MCP hosts establish connections to multiple MCP servers through dedicated MCP clients. The architecture consists of two layers: a data layer implementing JSON-RPC 2.0-based communication with lifecycle management and core features (tools, resources, prompts, notifications), and a transport layer managing communication channels and authentication through mechanisms such as Stdio and Streamable HTTP transport.

entries.title: What is Agent Communication Protocol (ACP)?
entries.subtitle: An open standard enabling interoperability and collaboration among siloed software agents.
entries.abstract: This explainer describes the Agent Communication Protocol (ACP), an open standard designed to facilitate agent-to-agent communication and enable interoperability across previously isolated agent systems. The document discusses how ACP can reduce integration barriers and promote collaborative agentic architectures. A prominent note indicates that ACP has been merged into the A2A standard under Linux Foundation stewardship, with the ACP team transitioning its technology and expertise accordingly, suggesting users consult official migration documentation.

entries.title: The AT Protocol Ethos
entries.subtitle: An examination of the philosophical and design principles underlying AT Protocol, a decentralized social media protocol influenced by the web, peer-to-peer, and open protocols movements.
entries.abstract: This text analyzes the foundational ethos and design principles of AT Protocol (atproto), a protocol for creating decentralized social applications. The author traces atproto's intellectual lineage to three technological movements: the document-based web with its permissionless architecture, the peer-to-peer movement's reaction against centralized platforms, and open protocol design traditions. The piece examines how atproto synthesizes these influences while introducing novel approaches to decentralized social media architecture, discussing both the innovations atproto contributes and the philosophical commitments underlying its design choices.

entries.title: What Do We Ask of Artificial Intelligence?
entries.subtitle: An examination of how societies must first determine the fundamental purpose and role of artificial intelligence systems before establishing effective governance frameworks.
entries.abstract: This essay argues that public discourse on artificial intelligence governance is disordered because it fails to address a prior fundamental question: what do we want artificial intelligence to be for us? The author identifies three broad possibilities for AI's role in society as superior reason/oracle, as reflexive tool subordinate to human knowledge, or as intermediate system stronger in narrow domains but subordinate to collective judgment. The essay contends that governance rules cannot be sensibly established without first clarifying society's intended purpose for these systems and its conception of human flourishing, drawing on philosophical and political theory to demonstrate that technological governance reflects deeper values about authority and the forms of life a society wishes to preserve.
entries.body:
What Do We Ask of Artificial Intelligence?
By: S.C. Langa

Public discourse around artificial intelligence often begins in the wrong place. We ask whether these systems should be regulated, whether they threaten jobs, whether they infringe upon intellectual property rights, and whether their underlying infrastructure places undue strain upon shared resources. These are important questions, but they all presume an earlier one that is more fundamental and far less settled: what, exactly, do we want artificial intelligence to be for us? Until that question is answered, disagreements about AI will remain disordered, because disputes over governance are often only disguised disputes over ends. A society cannot sensibly decide how to constrain a tool when it has not first decided what that tool is meant to do.

Today, at least three broad possibilities present themselves. The first is that artificial intelligence should surpass ordinary human limitations and help guide social life through superior inference, prediction, and judgment. The second is that it should remain primarily reflexive, preserving and reproducing human knowledge without occupying a higher authority than the people who made it. The third is that it should occupy an intermediate space: stronger than the individual in narrow domains, yet always subordinate to collective human judgment and political control. Each of these possibilities carries with it a different image of human flourishing, a different account of authority, and a different vision of what kind of society we are trying to build.

Before one can argue about whether artificial intelligence ought to be feared, regulated, or welcomed, one must first be clear about what is being discussed. For the purposes of this essay, artificial intelligence does not mean every digital tool, but a machine-based system that, for explicit or implicit objectives, infers from inputs how to generate outputs such as predictions, recommendations, decisions, or content capable of influencing real or virtual environments (OECD; NIST). Governance, in turn, should not be understood merely as post hoc regulation, but as a broader set of legal, political, and ethical constraints through which a society directs how such systems are designed, deployed, and overseen (NIST). And if this essay asks what we want AI for, it is ultimately asking about societyвЂ™s account of human flourishing: not efficiency alone, but what the Aristotelian tradition calls eudaimonia, or living well in the fullest sense (Kraut). Until these terms are clarified, much of the public argument about AI remains confused, because disputes that appear to concern rules often conceal a deeper disagreement about ends.

If disputes around artificial intelligence often conceal a deeper disagreement about ends, then it follows the argument that rules alone cannot settle the matter. Rules are not self-justifying. Instead, they arise from prior judgments about purpose, harm, and the forms of life a society wishes to preserve. Contemporary AI governance frameworks already reflect this logic. NIST, for example, notes that responsible AI practices help align decisions about design, development, and use with a systemвЂ™s вЂњintended aim and values,вЂќ and it further explains that the identification of purposes is among the early decisions that shape later judgments about risk and even whether deployment should proceed at all (NIST 1, 23). This is not merely administrative common sense; it is a political truth. As Langdon Winner argues, technologies are not to be judged only by their efficiency or productivity, but also by the ways they can вЂњembody specific forms of power and authorityвЂќ (121). Once that is granted, it becomes difficult to pretend that AI can be regulated sensibly before society has decided whether it wants these systems to remain instruments, become advisors, or assume some more authoritative place in human affairs. Even fear becomes unstable when purpose is left undefined, for what one person dreads as domination another welcomes as progress, and what one calls safety another calls stagnation. The disorder of public argument, then, lies not simply in disagreement over policy, but in the fact that we have too rarely said aloud what, in the first place, we want these systems to be for us.

The first and perhaps most ambitious model is that artificial intelligence ought to function as a form of superior reason: not merely as an instrument, but as a system whose predictive and inferential capacities exceed those of ordinary human beings to such a degree that it begins to resemble an oracle. On this view, the appeal of AI lies not simply in speed or convenience, but in the possibility that some forms of human error, bias, inconsistency, and ignorance might be mitigated by a more powerful intelligence. Bostrom captures the outer edge of this aspiration when he defines a superintelligence as an intellect that вЂњgreatly exceeds the cognitive performance of humans in virtually all domains of interestвЂќ (22). Yet even if such a system were possible, the political question would remain unresolved. Greater intelligence is not identical with rightful rule. As Christiano notes, political authority concerns the normative power to impose duties and command obedience, not merely the possession of superior judgment (вЂњAuthorityвЂќ). The dream of AI as superior reason therefore contains a hidden leap: it assumes that better prediction may license greater authority. That assumption is precisely what must be examined rather than smuggled in. For if, as Winner argues, technologies can embody вЂњspecific forms of power and authority,вЂќ then the desire for an artificial oracle is not only a technical ambition but a constitutional one, since it asks whether a society is willing to vest meaningful authority in a system it did not merely build, but may come to trust more than itself (121).

The second possibility is that artificial intelligence should remain primarily reflexive, inheriting and reproducing human knowledge without claiming a higher authority than the people who made it. In this model, AI is valuable not because it transcends the human world, but because it preserves and rearticulates it at scale. Such systems would function less as oracles than as mirrors: they would organize, summarize, and recombine the language, judgments, and patterns already present in human life. There is an apparent modesty in this vision. A reflexive system may seem easier to govern precisely because it does not pretend to be sovereign. Yet its modesty is deceptive. A mirror does not only return what is best in a society; it also returns what is distorted, partial, and unjust. NISTвЂ™s bias guidance notes that in AI systems, biases can arise from the datasets and algorithmic processes used in development, including historical and representational distortions that are already present in the social world (Schwartz et al. 8). Bender et al. make a parallel point in linguistic terms when they warn that large language models are trained to reproduce patterns in data without any grounding in meaning, making them capable of reassembling human discourse without understanding it (вЂњStochastic ParrotsвЂќ 614). And as NobleвЂ™s work on search demonstrates, systems that appear merely to retrieve and rank information can still reproduce and amplify existing social hierarchies rather than neutrally reflect reality (Noble). The promise of AI as mirror, then, is also its danger: a society may believe it has built something modest and familiar, only to discover that it has automated its own habits, prejudices, and limitations back onto itself.

The third possibility is that artificial intelligence should function as a form of bounded augmentation: more capable than the individual human being in narrow and useful domains, yet always constrained by human purposes, human oversight, and political judgment. This model rejects both the fantasy of artificial sovereignty and the false modesty of mere reflection. Its aim is not to create a machine that rules, nor one that simply echoes the past, but one that extends human capability while remaining answerable to human ends. There is growing support for such an approach in contemporary governance and design frameworks. The OECDвЂ™s AI Principles insist that AI actors should respect вЂњhuman-centred values and fairness,вЂќ including dignity, autonomy, and democratic values (OECD). NIST similarly emphasizes the need to define roles and responsibilities within вЂњhuman-AI team configurationsвЂќ and to maintain oversight of system performance (NIST 26). From the perspective of design, Ben Shneiderman argues that well-designed systems can combine вЂњhigh levels of human control and high levels of computer automation,вЂќ thereby increasing performance while preserving human self-efficacy, creativity, and responsibility (2). Yet the attractiveness of this middle path should not obscure its difficulty. To keep AI bounded requires more than goodwill. It requires institutions capable of deciding where automation should end, where human review must remain meaningful rather than ceremonial, and what kinds of dependence a free society ought never to permit. The question is therefore not whether bounded augmentation is appealing, but whether we possess the civic discipline required to keep it bounded.

What ultimately distinguishes these three models from one another, is not only the power they grant to artificial intelligence, but the kind of human being each one presupposes and helps produce. The question is not merely whether AI will change work, but what it will make of workers, citizens, and creators. Philosophers of labor have long argued that work matters not only because it secures income, but because it helps structure agency, social standing, and the pursuit of a meaningful life (Cholbi). From that perspective, the danger of AI is not exhausted by the prospect of unemployment. Even where labor remains, it may be thinned into supervision, correction, and procedural assent: the human being becomes less a maker than a monitor. The oracle model risks weakening judgment by encouraging deference to systems thought to know better than we do. The mirror model risks dulling creativity by returning inherited patterns in ever more polished form, making repetition easier than thought. Even the model of bounded augmentation, attractive though it is, leaves unresolved the question of responsibility, for as Mark Coeckelbergh notes, the use of AI complicates responsibility attribution even when humans remain, in principle, the responsible agents (55, 95). If human flourishing involves not simply efficiency but the exercised capacities to judge, create, answer for oneвЂ™s actions, and participate meaningfully in a shared world, then the question of AI becomes inseparable from the question of what forms of life we are willing to let technology reorganize.

What this suggests, in the end, is that the question of artificial intelligence is fundamentally philosophical before it is technical, and civic before it is commercial. Public debate often treats AI as though its chief problems were matters of engineering capacity, market adoption, or regulatory timing. Those questions matter, but they come later. Before a society can decide how much intelligence it wants, how much automation it can tolerate, or how much risk it is willing to bear, it must first decide what ends it is prepared to order these systems toward. That is why the debate cannot be settled by benchmarks alone, nor by efficiency gains, nor by the mere fact that a technology can be built. As the OECDвЂ™s principles insist, AI governance ultimately turns on human-centred values, fairness, dignity, autonomy, and democratic commitments (OECD). NIST similarly frames AI risk and oversight in relation to intended aims, values, and human roles rather than treating technical performance as sufficient in itself (NIST 1, 23, 26). The real difficulty, then, is not simply building powerful systems, but remaining politically and morally clear about what should guide them, what should limit them, and what must remain meaningfully human. The question of AI is therefore not only what these systems can do, but what kind of life their adoption invites us to accept.


Works Cited

Cholbi, Michael. вЂњPhilosophical Approaches to Work and Labor.вЂќ The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, 2022, plato.stanford.edu/entries/work-labor/. Accessed 23 Mar. 2026.

entries.title: The Silicon Valley Canon: On the PaД±deia of the American Tech Elite
entries.abstract: This essay argues that Silicon Valley has a real, if informal, intellectual canon, and that the books its elites read help shape not just their ideas but their moral formation, ambitions, and sense of what kind of people ought to lead society. Framed through the classical idea of paideiaвЂ”education as the formation of characterвЂ”the author contrasts WashingtonвЂ™s narrow, credential-driven culture of expertise with Silicon ValleyвЂ™s self-image as a world of high-agency generalists who prize intelligence, action, and sweeping first-principles thinking. He then uses a crowd-sourced list of influential tech books, from Foundation and Atlas Shrugged to Seeing Like a State, The Sovereign Individual, and Zero to One, to show how the ValleyвЂ™s reading habits reveal a distinctive worldview: skeptical of authority, fascinated by systems and history-making individuals, and drawn to ideas that feel both grand and actionable. It is a sharp, culturally observant piece that will especially appeal to readers interested in how the reading life of an elite class quietly shapes the politics, instincts, and blind spots of American tech power.

entries.title: Why "AI Safety" and "AI Regulation" Are Not the Same Conversation
entries.subtitle: The conflation of technical safety research with policy regulation has muddied both fields.
entries.excerpt: The conflation of technical safety research with policy regulation has muddied both fields. They share concerns but operate on different timescales, with different tools, and for different audiences. Technical AI safety asks: how do we build systems that reliably do what we intend? AI regulation asks: who bears responsibility when they don't? These are related but distinct projects, and treating them as identical has real costs for how we allocate attention, assign institutional responsibility, and govern a technology we do not yet fully understand.
entries.abstract: The conflation of technical AI safety research with AI policy regulation has muddied both fields. They share concerns ГѓВѓГ‚ВѓГѓВ‚Г‚ВўГѓВѓГ‚В‚ГѓВ‚Г‚ВЂГѓВѓГ‚В‚ГѓВ‚Г‚В” neither wants AI systems that cause catastrophic harm ГѓВѓГ‚ВѓГѓВ‚Г‚ВўГѓВѓГ‚В‚ГѓВ‚Г‚ВЂГѓВѓГ‚В‚ГѓВ‚Г‚В” but they operate on different timescales, with different tools, for different audiences. This essay argues for a cleaner separation, and explains why conflating them produces worse outcomes in both domains.

entries.title: The Problem with "Technology-Neutral" Legal Frameworks
entries.subtitle: Regulators and courts often aspire to write technology-neutral rules ГѓВѓГ‚ВѓГѓВ‚Г‚ВўГѓВѓГ‚В‚ГѓВ‚Г‚ВЂГѓВѓГ‚В‚ГѓВ‚Г‚В” this aspiration frequently produces worse outcomes.
entries.excerpt: Regulators and courts often aspire to write technology-neutral rules, believing that the law should describe conduct rather than tools. The argument here is that this aspiration, while understandable, frequently produces worse outcomes than honest technology-specific reasoning. When the underlying technology structures the nature of the harm, pretending otherwise in the legal framework doesn't produce neutrality ГѓВѓГ‚ВѓГѓВ‚Г‚ВўГѓВѓГ‚В‚ГѓВ‚Г‚ВЂГѓВѓГ‚В‚ГѓВ‚Г‚В” it produces confusion.
entries.abstract: Technology-neutral legal drafting is an aspiration with genuine appeal: rules that do not privilege or penalize specific technologies seem fairer and more durable. This essay argues that in practice, the aspiration frequently produces worse outcomes than honest technology-specific reasoning.
entries.preview: A sharp argument that technology-neutral legal drafting often produces worse outcomes than honest technology-specific reasoning ГѓВѓГ‚ВѓГѓВ‚Г‚ВўГѓВѓГ‚В‚ГѓВ‚Г‚ВЂГѓВѓГ‚В‚ГѓВ‚Г‚В” and that courts invoking the principle are frequently using it to avoid hard choices.

entries.title: On the Poverty of "Move Fast and Fix It Later" as Governance Philosophy
entries.subtitle: A critique of the regulatory philosophy that treats market deployment as the primary mechanism for identifying harm.
entries.excerpt: A critique of the regulatory philosophy that treats market deployment as the primary mechanism for identifying harm. The populations this approach systematically disadvantages are not random ГѓВѓГ‚ВѓГѓВ‚Г‚ВўГѓВѓГ‚В‚ГѓВ‚Г‚ВЂГѓВѓГ‚В‚ГѓВ‚Г‚В” they are the ones least positioned to exit the market, least likely to be heard in retrospective processes, and most exposed to the harms that deployment surfaces. Speed has costs that are not borne equally.
entries.abstract: The "move fast and fix it later" philosophy treats market deployment as the primary mechanism for surfacing harm ГѓВѓГ‚ВѓГѓВ‚Г‚ВўГѓВѓГ‚В‚ГѓВ‚Г‚ВЂГѓВѓГ‚В‚ГѓВ‚Г‚В” the assumption being that if something causes serious damage, we will notice and correct it. This essay argues that this assumption is false for a specific and important category of harms: those that are diffuse, slow-moving, or concentrated among populations with limited political voice.

entries.title: What Open Source Actually Means in the Context of AI Models
entries.subtitle: The word "open" is doing enormous work in AI policy debates.
entries.excerpt: The word "open" is doing enormous work in AI policy debates, often without examination. Releasing model weights is not the same as releasing training data. Releasing training data is not the same as releasing architecture decisions. This essay tries to disaggregate the term and ask which version of openness actually matters for accountability, for access, and for the kind of public oversight that motivated open source as a principle in the first place.
entries.abstract: "Open source" in the context of AI models can mean releasing weights, releasing training code, releasing training data, or some combination. This essay disaggregates the term and asks which version of openness actually matters for accountability, access, and safety.
entries.preview: A careful disaggregation of what 'open source' actually means when applied to AI models, and which version of openness matters for the policy goals most commonly invoked in its defense.

entries.title: The Institutional Gap in AI Governance
entries.subtitle: The question is not just what rules we need, but what kinds of institutions can credibly enforce them.
entries.excerpt: Existing regulatory institutions were not designed for general-purpose technologies that operate across every sector simultaneously. The question is not just what rules we need, but what kinds of institutions can credibly enforce them, accumulate the expertise to understand what they are regulating, and adapt fast enough to remain relevant.
entries.abstract: Existing regulatory institutions were not designed for general-purpose technologies that operate across every sector simultaneously. This essay examines the institutional design challenges posed by AI governance and considers what kinds of organizations are actually capable of doing the work.

entries.title: Liability Without Causation: The Challenge of Algorithmic Harm
entries.subtitle: Traditional tort doctrine requires a clear causal chain. Algorithmic systems fracture that chain.
entries.excerpt: Traditional tort doctrine requires a clear causal chain between conduct and harm. Algorithmic systems fracture that chain into statistical correlations, distributed decision-making, and emergent behavior that no single actor chose or foresaw. This essay asks what a liability framework that can handle these properties would actually need to look like.
entries.abstract: Traditional tort doctrine requires a clear causal chain between conduct and harm. Algorithmic systems fracture that chain into statistical correlations, distributed decision-making, and emergent behavior that no single actor chose or foresaw. This essay asks what a liability framework that can handle these properties would actually need to look like.
